1: Joel is undated — no contemporary king is mentioned — but it is typically placed in the post-exilic period (late 5th-early 4th century BCE) based on its references to the temple cult and its post-exilic vocabulary. The book's structure: a devastating locust plague (Joel 1-Joel 2:11) serves as the occasion for a call to repentance (Joel 2:12-17), followed by YHWH's promise of restoration (Joel 2:18-3:21). The locust plague is both a literal agricultural disaster and the harbinger of the Day of the LORD — the pattern of divine judgment working through historical events that will culminate in the final eschatological judgment. Joel is the OT prophet most cited in the NT on the Day of Pentecost, making his prophecy of the Spirit's universal outpouring the bridge between the Old and New covenant eras.
2: 'Has such a thing happened in your days, or in the days of your fathers?' The call to generational memory is a rhetorical device establishing the unprecedented scale of the locust disaster. Agricultural memory in the ancient Near East was long — oral traditions preserved accounts of famines and plagues across generations. The comparison 'tell your children' invokes the Passover-instruction pattern (Exodus 12:26-27, Joel 13:8), repurposing the command to transmit salvation-history for transmitting judgment-history.
3: 'Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children, and their children to another generation.' The generational transmission formula — this plague is to be remembered alongside the Exodus and other covenant-defining events. The three-generation chain (your children → their children → another generation) parallels the way Deuteronomy instructs covenant memory (Deuteronomy 4:9-10, Joel 6:2). A locust plague severe enough to be preserved in generational memory was genuinely catastrophic — modern locust swarms in the Middle East have stripped regions of all vegetation within hours.
4: 'What the cutting locust left, the swarming locust has eaten. What the swarming locust left, the hopping locust has eaten, and what the hopping locust left, the destroying locust has eaten.' The four locust terms — gazam (cutting/gnawing), arbeh (swarming, the common Hebrew word for locust), yeleq (hopping/young locust), chasil (destroying/finishing) — have been variously interpreted as four distinct species, four stages of locust development, or a poetic series emphasizing total destruction. Modern entomologists recognize that desert locust swarms (Schistocerca gregaria) can contain 40-80 million insects per square kilometer, stripping 100,000 tons of vegetation per day.
5: 'Awake, you drunkards, and weep, and wail, all you drinkers of wine, because of the sweet wine, for it is cut off from your mouth.' The drunkards are addressed first because the wine harvest is the most visible casualty of the locust plague — the grape vines stripped bare. The 'sweet wine' (asis, new wine, grape juice) represents the entire harvest cycle: if the vines are destroyed, there will be no wine that season. Addressing drunkards is also a rhetorical device: even those whose primary interest is self-indulgence should be awakened by this disaster.
6: 'For a nation has come up against my land, powerful and beyond number; its teeth are like lions' teeth, and it has the fangs of a lioness.' The locusts are metaphorized as an invading nation (goy) — a military force without number. The lion-teeth image precisely describes locust mandibles: locusts have serrated, V-shaped mandibles capable of biting through dry stalks rapidly. The comparison to lion and lioness teeth (shineihu shinei aryeh veme'talot laviy lo) is also in Proverbs 30:14, applied to the wicked. The personification of the locust swarm as a foreign army with predatory jaws sets up Joel 2's extended military metaphor.
7: 'It has laid waste my vine and splintered my fig tree; it has stripped off their bark and thrown it down; their branches are made white.' The vine and fig tree were the two most economically and symbolically important agricultural products of ancient Palestine — a standard symbol for peace and prosperity (Micah 4:4: 'every man under his vine and under his fig tree'). The 'white branches' (sarugeiha hivinu) — the peeled, whitened wood after the bark is stripped — is a precisely observed detail of locust damage; the skeletal white branches are the final visible result of total stripping.
8: 'Lament like a virgin wearing sackcloth for the bridegroom of her youth.' The mourning image: a young woman lamenting the death of her betrothed before the marriage was consummated. In the ancient Levant, betrothal carried strong legal and emotional weight — the betrothed couple was considered 'husband and wife' legally (Deuteronomy 22:23-24) even before the wedding. The mourning posture — wearing sackcloth — is the standard grief garment of the OT (Genesis 37:34, 2 Samuel 3:31, Isaiah 22:12). The intensity of this grief is the measure of the community's appropriate response to the disaster.
9: 'The grain offering and the drink offering are cut off from the house of the LORD; the priests mourn, the ministers of the LORD.' The daily tamid offering (the perpetual sacrifice, Exodus 29:38-42, Numbers 28:3-8) required grain and wine: two lambs per day, with grain and oil, plus wine. When the locust plague destroyed the crops, the material basis for temple worship was removed. The cessation of the tamid was considered one of the gravest possible covenant crises — Daniel 9:27, Joel 11:31, 12:11 use its 'removal' as a sign of ultimate desolation, and 1 Macc 1:45 records Antiochus IV forbidding it as the defining act of desecration.
10: 'The fields are destroyed, the ground mourns, because the grain is destroyed, the wine dries up, the oil languishes.' The three staple crops of Palestinian agriculture: grain (dagan), wine (tirosh), and oil (yitzhar) — the triad that represents covenant blessing in Deuteronomy (Joel 7:13, Joel 11:14, Joel 12:17). The 'mourning' of the ground (avela ha-adamah) is a personification — land mourning appears in Hosea 4:3 and Jeremiah 12:4, Joel 23:10. Ancient Israelites understood the land as a covenant participant: when the covenant is broken, the land itself is affected (Leviticus 26:20, 32-35).
11: 'Be ashamed, O tillers of the soil; wail, O vinedressers, for the wheat and the barley, because the harvest of the field has perished.' The shame (hobbishu) of farmers is the shame of skilled workers whose craft has become futile — the land has rejected their labor. Wheat (chitah) and barley (se'orah) were the two main grain crops: wheat for bread, barley for poorer bread and animal feed. The 'harvest of the field' (qetzir sadeh) represents the entire year's agricultural output — not just a single crop.
12: 'The vine dries up; the fig tree languishes. Pomegranate, palm, and apple — all the trees of the field are dried up, and gladness dries up from the children of man.' The enumeration of fruit trees — vine (gefen), fig (te'enah), pomegranate (rimon), date palm (tamar), apple (tappuach) — is a catalog of Palestinian horticulture. These slow-growing trees represent multi-generational investment; their destruction is catastrophic beyond a single season. 'Gladness dries up from the children of man' (yavesh sos min-benei adam) — joy is agricultural in the ancient world; when the harvest fails, the social fabric of celebration collapses.
13: 'Put on sackcloth and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar. Go in, pass the night in sackcloth, O ministers of my God, because the grain offering and the drink offering are withheld from the house of your God.' The priests are given a specific instruction: 'lie in sackcloth all night' (linu basaqim) — overnight lament before YHWH, a form of extended mourning vigil. The dual address 'my God... your God' moves between divine and human perspective: YHWH calls the priests 'ministers of my God' (YHWH speaking), then they are instructed about 'the house of your God' — the same temple from both perspectives.
14: 'Consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly. Gather the elders and all the inhabitants of the land to the house of the LORD your God, and cry out to the LORD.' The national covenant ceremony (miqra qodesh, solemn assembly) was the standard community crisis response in ancient Israel — cf. 2 Chronicles 20:3-4 (Jehoshaphat's crisis fast), Ezra 8:21-23 (Ezra's fast before returning from Babylon). The gathering of 'elders and all inhabitants' parallels Deuteronomy 31:12: 'assemble the people, men, women, and little ones, and the sojourner within your towns.' The solemn assembly activates the covenant machinery of corporate petition.
15: 'Alas for the day! For the day of the LORD is near, and as destruction from the Almighty it comes.' First mention of the key theological concept: the Day of YHWH (yom YHWH). The concept appears in Amos 5:18-20, Isaiah 2:12, Zephaniah 1:14-18, and Ezekiel 7:10 — a day when YHWH intervenes in history with decisive judgment. The wordplay on 'Almighty' is characteristic: 'destruction from the Almighty' (ushod mishadday yavo) — shod (destruction) echoes Shadday (Almighty, El Shaddai). Amos (Joel 5:18-20) had warned that the Day of YHWH would be darkness, not light, for Israel — Joel develops this warning with the locust as its embodiment.
16: 'Is not the food cut off before our eyes, joy and gladness from the house of our God?' The rhetorical question makes visible the connection between material loss and spiritual crisis. 'Before our eyes' (le-neinu min-beyt Eloheinu) — the loss is visible, undeniable. The 'house of our God' is the temple, the center of national worship and celebration. The three pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles) were occasions of communal joy (simchah); when the agricultural basis for those celebrations is destroyed, the joy is 'cut off' (nigzar) as surely as the crops.
17: 'The seed shrivels under the clods; the storehouses are desolate; the granaries are torn down because the grain has dried up.' Specific agricultural detail: seed planted in the ground (parot tachath megrephotam) — the seeds have dried up under their clods, failing to germinate or withered before germination. The 'storehouses' (mammegarot) and 'granaries' (asamim) torn down because they have nothing to protect. Ancient storage pits and granary buildings were large community infrastructure investments; emptying and abandoning them represents the total collapse of the agricultural system.
18: 'How the beasts groan! The herds of cattle are perplexed because there is no pasture for them; even the flocks of sheep suffer.' The suffering of animals during a locust plague is ecologically precise: when locusts strip the vegetation, domestic animals have no pasture. The 'perplexity' (nashemu, desolated/confused) of cattle is a strong verb — large animals that normally exhibit calm purposefulness are disoriented. Ancient Israelite agriculture was mixed: grain, horticulture, and herding were integrated, so the loss of pasture compounds the loss of grain.
19: 'To you, O LORD, I call. For fire has devoured the pastures of the wilderness, and flame has burned all the trees of the field.' The prophet's prayer begins here — shifting from description (vv1-18) to petition. 'Fire' (esh) devouring the pastures may be literal (fires that sometimes accompany or follow locust invasions when dry vegetation burns) or metaphorical (the locusts' effect visually resembling scorched earth). The 'pastures of the wilderness' (ne'ot midbar) are the uncultivated grazing lands — even the areas too marginal for agriculture are stripped.
20: 'Even the beasts of the field pant for you because the water brooks are dried up, and fire has devoured the pastures of the wilderness.' The wild animals join the community of petition — they 'pant for' YHWH (ta'arog eleykha) as Psalms 42:1 uses the same word for the deer panting for water. The drying of the water brooks (aphikey mayim) alongside the locust damage represents a compound disaster: locust plague and drought together. The inclusion of wild animals in the prayer reflects the OT's view of all creation as within YHWH's covenant care (cf. Psalms 36:6, Jonah 4:11).
